revision.text: {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].

{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] developed in the 660s. The kokufu replaced the older ''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]]''.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.

== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] developed in the 660s. The kokufu replaced the older ''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]]''.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.

revision.comment: /* History */
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] developed in the 660s. The kokufu replaced the older ''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]]''.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho||守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|Fuchū|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{Literatur|Autor=Akira Imatani|Titel=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|Übersetzer=Suzanne Gay|Herausgeber=Kōzō Yamamura|Sammelwerk=The Cambridge History of Japan|Band=Volume 3: Medieval Japan|Verlag=Cambridge University Press|Jahr=2003|ISBN=0-521-22354-7|Seiten=251|Online={{Google Buch|BuchID=TtTc_Aa22MwC|Seite=251}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{Literatur|Autor=Ryō Kinoshita|Titel={{lang|ja|古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について}} / Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|Sammelwerk={{lang|ja|国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告}} / Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|ISSN=0286-7400|Nummer=10|Monat=März|Jahr=1986|Seiten=337|Online=[http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf englischer Abstract]}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{Literatur|Autor=Martin Schwind|Titel=Das Japanische Inselreich|Band=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|Verlag=de Gruyter|Ort=Berlin|Jahr=1981|ISBN=3-11-008319-1|Seiten=69–70|Online={{Google Buch|BuchID=nq-HuwA32twC|Seite=69}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{Literatur|Autor=Atsuru Yagi|Titel={{lang|ja|国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－}} / Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|Sammelwerk={{lang|ja|国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告}} / Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|ISSN=0286-7400|Nummer=10|Monat=März|Jahr=1986|Seiten=343|Online=[http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf englischer Abstract]}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: /* Organisation */
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] developed in the 660s. The kokufu replaced the older ''[[Kuni no Miyatsuko]]''.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho||守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|Fuchū|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang Dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{Literatur|Autor=Akira Imatani|Titel=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|Übersetzer=Suzanne Gay|Herausgeber=Kōzō Yamamura|Sammelwerk=The Cambridge History of Japan|Band=Volume 3: Medieval Japan|Verlag=Cambridge University Press|Jahr=2003|ISBN=0-521-22354-7|Seiten=251|Online={{Google Buch|BuchID=TtTc_Aa22MwC|Seite=251}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{Literatur|Autor=Ryō Kinoshita|Titel={{lang|ja|古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について}} / Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|Sammelwerk={{lang|ja|国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告}} / Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|ISSN=0286-7400|Nummer=10|Monat=März|Jahr=1986|Seiten=337|Online=[http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf englischer Abstract]}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{Literatur|Autor=Martin Schwind|Titel=Das Japanische Inselreich|Band=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|Verlag=de Gruyter|Ort=Berlin|Jahr=1981|ISBN=3-11-008319-1|Seiten=69–70|Online={{Google Buch|BuchID=nq-HuwA32twC|Seite=69}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{Literatur|Autor=Atsuru Yagi|Titel={{lang|ja|国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－}} / Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|Sammelwerk={{lang|ja|国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告}} / Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|ISSN=0286-7400|Nummer=10|Monat=März|Jahr=1986|Seiten=343|Online=[http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf englischer Abstract]}}</ref> </references>

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho||守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|Fuchū|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang Dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{Literatur|Autor=Akira Imatani|Titel=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|Übersetzer=Suzanne Gay|Herausgeber=Kōzō Yamamura|Sammelwerk=The Cambridge History of Japan|Band=Volume 3: Medieval Japan|Verlag=Cambridge University Press|Jahr=2003|ISBN=0-521-22354-7|Seiten=251|Online={{Google Buch|BuchID=TtTc_Aa22MwC|Seite=251}}}}</ref>

Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} are the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645) which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho||守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|Fuchū|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang Dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|others=Translated by Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=Volume 3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|ISBN=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang Dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=Volume 3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|ISBN=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

revision.comment: 26 revisions imported from [[:wikipedia:Kokufu]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] to [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
{{Authority control}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia01]] to [[Category:dd]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
{{Authority control}}

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
{{Authority control}}

<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
{{Authority control}}

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

[[Category:Historical geography of Japan]] [[Category:Capitals in Asia]] [[en:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kokufu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]]

{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Redirect]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

[[Category:Historical geography of Japan]] [[Category:Capitals in Asia]] [[en:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Kokufu]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]] [[Category:New]]

{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />

When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.

However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.

With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />

revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
