revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:New]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Bot: append Wikidata-derived interwikis
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] to [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[Provinces of Japan]] from the [[Nara period]] to the [[Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[Buddhist]] provincial temples, ''[[kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Bot: prepend Wikidata AFC comment via pages.txt
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[commons:" to "[[sn:commons:"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata has English label - based on Wikidata English label
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

{{wikidata link|Q1629088}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical [[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces of Japan]] from the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] to the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]], replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]''), the provincial governments (''[[:en:Kokuga|Kokuga]]'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the [[:en:Capital of Japan|Capital of Japan]], first [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]] and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang]] capital [[:en:Chang'an|Chang'an]]. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of [[:en:Suō Province|Suō Province]] occupied an area of 1 km², that of [[:en:Bizen Province|Bizen Province]] 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Wikidata has English label]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1629088]] Wikidata name: kokufu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan}} from the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=kokushi}}, replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}''), the provincial governments (''{{ill|Kokuga|en|Kokuga|WD=Q11418465|lt=Kokuga}}'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}}, first {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|WD=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō}} and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}} capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an}}. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|WD=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province}} occupied an area of 1 km², that of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan}} from the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} to the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=kokushi}}, replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}''), the provincial governments (''{{ill|Kokuga|en|Kokuga|WD=Q11418465|lt=Kokuga}}'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan}}, first {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|WD=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō}} and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang}} capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an}}. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|WD=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province}} occupied an area of 1 km², that of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province}} 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Provincial capitals of Japan]][[de:Kokufu]][[en:Kokufu]][[fa:کوکوفو]][[fr:Kokufu]][[ja:国府]][[ko:국부 (일본사)]][[zh:國府 (日本)]]
{{nihongo|'''Kokufu'''|国府}} were the capitals of the historical {{ill|Provinces of Japan|en|Provinces of Japan|WD=Q860290|lt=Provinces of Japan|1=Provinces of Japan}} from the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} to the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
== History == As part of the [[Taika Reform]] (645), which aimed at a centralization of the administration following the Chinese model (''[[ritsuryō]]''), the kokufu and with it the office of the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}}, replacing the older ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'', developed in the 660s.<ref name="Yagi" />
The ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'' (Collection of Japanese Names) from 935 contains the earliest listing of the capitals of the provinces and their location. The location of the original capitals of the 8th and 9th century are not passed down.<ref name="Kinoshita" />
When during the [[:en:Muromachi Period|Muromachi Period]], starting in the 14th century, the functions of the kokushi were increasingly transferred to military governors (''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}''), the provincial governments (''{{ill|Kokuga|en|Kokuga|WD=Q11418465|lt=Kokuga|1=Kokuga}}'') lost their importance.
== Organisation == In the center of the kokufu lay the provincial government (''kokuga'') with its offices (administration, farming, finance, police and military) and the official building of the kokushi, known as {{nihongo|kokuchō|国庁}}.<ref name="Schwind" /> In the periphery there was the provincial school (''kokugaku''), the garrison and storehouses for taxes which were paid in kind.<ref name="Schwind" />
When the shugo replaced the kokushi, their administration, the {{nihongo|shugosho|守護所}} was occasionally found in or near the buildings of the kokuga. In these cases their administration was also referred to as {{nihongo|[[:en:Fuchū (disambiguation)|Fuchū]]|府中}}.<ref name="Imatani" />
With their square layout, the kokufu followed that of the {{ill|Capital of Japan|en|Capital of Japan|WD=Q1025961|lt=Capital of Japan|1=Capital of Japan}}, first {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|WD=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō|1=Fujiwara-kyō}} and then [[Heijō-kyō]], which in turn were modelled on the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang|1=Tang dynasty}} capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an|1=Chang'an}}. However, with exception of [[:en:Dazaifu (term)|Dazaifu]], which held a special position, these capitals were relatively small. For instance, the capital of {{ill|Suō Province|en|Suō Province|WD=Q1053037|lt=Suō Province|1=Suō Province}} occupied an area of 1 km², that of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|Bizen Province|WD=Q1053688|lt=Bizen Province|1=Bizen Province}} 850 m×850 m.<ref name="Schwind" />
Inside or near the kokufu, there were also the most important religious sites of the province, the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} provincial temples, ''[[:en:Kokubun-ji|Kokubun-ji]]'', one each for monks and nuns as well as the highest Ranked [[Shinto]] shrine, ''[[Ichinomiya]]''.<ref name="Schwind" />
==References== <references> <ref name="Imatani">{{citation|author=Akira Imatani|title=Muromachi local government: ''shugo'' and ''kokujin''|translator=Suzanne Gay|editor=Kōzō Yamamura|work=The Cambridge History of Japan|volume=3: Medieval Japan|publisher=Cambridge University Press|year=2003|isbn=0-521-22354-7|pages=251|url={{Google books|id=TtTc_Aa22MwC|page=251|plainurl=yes}}}}</ref>
<ref name="Kinoshita">{{citation|author=Ryō Kinoshita|chapter=古辞書類に見る国府所在郡について|trans-chapter=Whereabouts of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals) Observed in The Lexicons|language=ja|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=337|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029184550/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010001.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref>
<ref name="Schwind">{{cite book|author=Martin Schwind|title=Das Japanische Inselreich|volume=Band 2: Kulturlandschaft, Wirtschaftsgroßmacht auf engem Raum|publisher=de Gruyter|location=Berlin|year=1981|isbn=3-11-008319-1|pages=69–70|url={{Google books|id=nq-HuwA32twC|page=69|plainurl=yes}}|language=de}}</ref>
<ref name="Yagi">{{citation|author=Atsuru Yagi|chapter=国府の成立と構造 －文献史料からみた－|language=ja|trans-chapter=Formation and Structure of Kokufu (Provincial Capitals)|script-title=ja:国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告|trans-title=Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History|issn=0286-7400|number=10|date=March 1986|pages=343|url=http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20131029194409/http://www.rekihaku.ac.jp/english/publication/titles/titles_pdf/010005.pdf|archivedate=2013-10-29}}</ref> </references>
